speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with William Burkland took place at his home near Deary on March 19th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager. When you started to work in the woods...

speaker: William Burkland
words: When I started to work in the woods, well, I started in the woods and I was a kid, but that was at home. I worked for Potlatch in 1936. No, wait now. In 1910. Nineteen ten I worked for Potlatch. Summer of 1910. But before that, we worked in 19. We worked at home, that is, logged to Dalber's. So I logged in the woods. I worked in the woods all my life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were young and learning, what did your father have you do?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Everything. Everything there was to do. Even skidding. I drove horses when I was 12 years old. I started driving horses. From then on, I took over the horses. When I was around 15, 16, I took over the horses. Tired them, my father didn't want to drive horses. So I got that job. And I liked it, so I stayed with it. I drove horses for over 40 years. Even all of the horses of my own since I was around, well, in the 20s, I think. I bought two coats, three year olds. Oh, they were younger than that. I bought one year old coats. I bought two of them in 19, when the pair was in Seattle. Summer when the pair was in Seattle. And I raced them and started working them when I was three years old, or when they were three years old. And I worked them all the time. I logged with them when they were four years old. I logged with them for a fella down below a dairy there, a fella named Asplen. In fact, he was Philip Asplen's half-brother. And he had a contract to log south of the dairy. And I worked there in 1912 for him. I worked all winter. That's the first winter I logged with that team. And then I worked in the woods ever since I remember. Sawed and swam skidded and drayed on. We used dray logging down in Dray Logging. And I did down to the mill, and I told you we was at Pete Talberg's Mill. We logged down there. And I didn't matter. As far back as I can remember, almost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say dray logging, what do you mean by that?

speaker: William Burkland
words: One sled. We logged on one sled, that's the dray hog. We drove one into the log behind. Did dray haul, the Potlatch, even with the Potlatch for a long time. Did dray hauled in the wintertime and dolly hauled in the summertime. Dolly had a two-wheel. They made the wheels out of logs and put, yeah, and ironed them with two... Bands. Bands, around. And then they used them. That was Dolly Logging. And then they rolled a log on there, put near balance, you see, on the bunk. It didn't drag very hard on. You could adjust that the way you wanted it. If it was steep downhill, you could let the log roll the log so it was heavier behind, dragging harder. And you wanted it light on behind where you put the log up ahead and almost balanced it. And they hauled them awfully big loads that way. We used horses. They logged that way lots of times in the summertime. Potlatch did. Before they started with the donkeys. And then they started with the donkeys and then they pulled in the whole tree. That's what I was working on in 1910. I worked on a donkey. We put in whole trees because I didn't... Well, I worked out in the woods too. I was sawing. And then I worked on the skid wave where they pulled them in. We had to... They pulled in the whole tree and then we had to measure it up and saw them off. By hand, me and my older brother and Bill Olsen... Bill Olsen was... Did you know Alfred Olsen and Troy?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I knew who he was.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah. You knew who he was? Yeah. Well, it was his brother. It was his older brother. He worked with us on the skid wave in 1910. And it was early in the spring and it was wet too. Logs were wet, the trees were wet, the ground was wet. And the sun got out awfully hot. And we had to... He went out for us. He was working there and it got too hot. He was the kind of a guy that couldn't sweat. And he went out and we had to drag him out to shade. And we hadn't worked and he laid there about two or three hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a big man? A big man?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, he wasn't a big man. He was just ordinary. But he couldn't sweat. He's one of them hidebound fellers, you know, that couldn't sweat. My brother and I, we sweat. We sweat a little. It just run. It was hot in there and then it walled out of water, you know, and then the sun was caught. It was suffocating. It wasn't that. But then he got over it after he laid there a while. He got over it and come up and said he was going to help us. So we put him on to measure and we took the saw. The saw. Oh, I'll tell you, that wood works, that's hard work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it rough?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it rough?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yes, it's hard work. You saw it by hand, you know. You stand there and it worked all day. Oh, that was hard work. I'll bet there was a lot of it too. Yeah, you ain't a kidding. There's a lot of it. There was about, well, when they logged, there was about two gangs as a team. To keep a team going. They didn't have too awfully far to skid where it took two gangs to keep them going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many men was in a gang?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, two men as a gang.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two sawyers?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, two sawyers. One on each side of the saw. And then there was two of them, two saws to keep a team going. Unless it was far skidding, but they didn't have it. They didn't skid very far when they skidded out to the, you see, they were on tracks out. Spurs, railroad spurs. And they didn't figure on, oh, I don't know, half a 40 or 80 rods or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then were they using dollies then?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, not when they skidded that way. The dollies, they were when they skidded further than that. They skidded about a quarter of a mile or half a mile. Then they used dollies. But that was in the summertime when it was dry. But they did use dres, even when it was wet. It didn't have to be snow. If it was the ground or wet, they used dres. And they put the log on a dre and balanced it and pulled it in that way. That's the way they logged before they got the donkeys and the cats. Well, the cats, they didn't get them until they were late.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you working by the day… yeah… you got so much a day… yeah.

speaker: William Burkland
words: That wasn't too much, about $2. There wasn't much money in it that entire day. We didn't have any money. I worked in the harvest field, not in the harvest field on the trash machine, for 35 days. Straight through driving the Garret team for $2 a day. We were up about 16 hours a day, too. We were up at 4 o 'clock in the morning and Harney's up. And lots of evenings we had to work in... Well, we didn't work in lamp light, but the soil was good. They had to have a lantern for them to see the soil in the evening.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The sack sewers?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Sacks sewers. Oh, yeah, it was all sack grain then. And the sack sewers had to work in lamp light. And we, of course, we didn't need that. The horses, they knew the track. They had been going there all day long, forward and backwards and forward and backwards. We were pulling grain, and headed grain upon a derrick. And then there was a feeder run back, and there was two men on there raking that into the feeder, getting it into the machine. That was... Them days the trash and machine had about... Oh, let's see... Two, four, six, twelve, fourteen men on the crew, on the machine crew. You were driving team. I was driving team. I was driving the derrick team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the derrick team do? What did the derrick team do?

speaker: William Burkland
words: They pulled in the headed grain from the stack. You know what a derrick fork is?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I've seen a picture of them.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, well, they pulled them back on the stack and put them down in the... In that headed grain. And then they motioned us to go ahead, and we pulled up that load, and it took them up in the mask, and then they tripped it. It came down on a great big platform. Oh, it was half as big as this room. That's where they dropped it down. There was a man on each side, raking that in. Into the... What I call it, the elevator, and it went in the machine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they decide whether they were going to thrash it all in a big pile like that, or whether they put it in bundles?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, they brought... They were out there with a header, a header bed, and then they had a header bed that was made something like a bundle rack only much bigger. And they hauled it into the stack, put it in there just like a stack, you know. You open a stack and you thrash it all. If they want any divisions, why they'd have to divide it before they come to their stack with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Divisions?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, if there's any division in the grain, it'd be done. It had to be divided before they come to the stack with it. And after it was in the stack, then it went in the machine, the same as all of them. Same way with bundles, you know. They stacked bundles, too. And after they started bundled thrashing. But down in the Kalu's country, down around Colton and Uniontown, and down in there, it was all headed and grained.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where you went to work? Yeah, that's where I went to work.

speaker: William Burkland
words: That's where I worked. Out of Genesee, I worked out at Uniontown, west of Genesee. You started on the Kendrick-Shawnhorse place. You know where that is?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, but I know a little bit the country. I don't know that particular place… big place?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, yeah. Well, they were all faces. No, the Kendrick-Shawnhorse place. That's right in the edge of Genesee. That's not a very big... That's 160 acres. And then it is... It is the... It is Charlie Shawnhorse and Fred Shawnhorse. And Floemers, they were all related. Fred and Charlie Shawnhorse, they were brothers. And then as a slumber, he was married to Shawnhorse, a girl. And then it was... Oh, I don't remember the names, well, and then through there...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you enjoy harvest? Yeah, I liked it.

speaker: William Burkland
words: It slept out in the straw stack. Oh, yeah, I liked that. You get so... you like some of them things, you know. Sleeping out in the straw pile and getting up before the morning. The engineer was up before that. Well, he got up before that, so he had a whistler, and he whistled, and you had to roll out. You hurt your ears, you leaders. Yes, I liked that. And them days, I wouldn't like it now. Them days, I liked it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the men work really hard on something like that?

speaker: William Burkland
words: I mean, I know you'd have to work pretty hard. They worked as hard as they wanted to. There's really no driving unless you had to... That was harder than the rest of the work around there, pitching bundles, then you had to pitch, you know, to beat everything to get that into the machine and get out in the field again. And some of them bundles were both long and heavy. So that was really hard to work… the header or the dirt team worked. That was easy work, but I was just a kid then. I was about 16 years old. So that wasn't hard work, but you had to be there all day, right in the dust, back of them teams, backward and forward and backward and forward and back. You went one time and the other feller went one time. And you come back and your forker, he took his fork and the other one he took his, two teams and two forkers. And you see when the header,… the stack N2, the N2, there was an opening in between the stacks. And one built up the stack from that way and the other one built it up from this way back. Well, it was really started, the stack was started from the dividing line. Then they built them up, so they were out there about 40 or 50 feet from each site. And that's where we had to pull them in then after that. That's big size stack. Oh yeah, that's a big stack there. They had big stacks, they were about eight or ten feet high and they must have been about eight or ten feet wide. Oh, I tell you, that was lots of grain. They had lots of grain out there. At the end of the union town, they were pretty big farms. Some of them were big farms and even 160 acres, you know. The way wheat grows out there, them years, I think they grow more wheat than they do now. It's more straw, anyway. And that made a lot of straw. You cut, your header cut down, the straws cut that high. The header, you know, it cut up pretty high on the straw, but then they left about that much to get it all the way they, all the heads where they had to leave about a foot and a half or something like that, a straw, 16 inches. And that all went into the stack. So that country around Genesee seemed pretty prosperous to you in those days. Oh, yes, yes. That was always a good country. That was the cream of the country around Genesee. They claimed there was somehow a little better than Genesee, but I tell you, that was awfully hard to beat between Genesee and Union Town. That was not a fertile country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did lots of guys come from over here to work over there… oh, yes.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yes, but near all the old timers worked over there. He worked over there in the harvest, and a lot of them now are like Arthur Burke's dad. He worked there, oh, for several years, but before my time. He worked there from the time he came. He came out to Genesee, and he worked around there for a long time, both in the harvest and in the spring work. People were out there in the spring work, too. You know, they worked a lot of men in them days. It was all horse work, and it was only about, oh, all the way from four to six horses for every teamster. And it wasn't very many teamsters that used six horses. They used three and four, and they had to have a man with every team. So there was a lot of man work them days. That's been done away, and they're doing away with it right along. The man later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was the outfit that you would, would you go work for the same outfit when you'd go over there?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, I worked for several outfits over there. This time when I was driving Dirk's team, I worked for Nils Johnson. He lived right out south of town here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He took his whole operation from here over there? Yeah, a lot of them, anyway. You mean his outfit?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, he did. Oh, he kept his outfit down there. He borrowed it at Genesee, and he borrowed it from Dick Schoenholst. And he always left it there when he worked there. He was down there about three or four years, I think. And he kept his machinery down there. And I worked for John Vedvik. He was from Troy. I worked for him after that. That was a bundle rig, though. And I worked for Halvorson. Halvorson and Johnson down there. I worked for them in the Thrashen. That was also a bundle rig. That was three years. Oh, no, there wasn't much more than that. I was out there in Hall of Rain one fall. Halvorson, Halvorson, Halvorson. He was a Genesee man. He lived beside a Genesee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So one of these crews, one of these outfits with all the men on it, would quite a few of the men in the outfit be Troy area men? Well, it was with- Deary area? It was with Vedvik. There was a lot of them.

speaker: William Burkland
words: There was quite a- Quite a- The whole crew were from Troy. And that was Austen. That was John Austen and Pete- Pete Svensen. And Charles Ronberg. And the fellow by the name of Alfred Westland. And John Estlen. I don't know if I can remember anything. I'm surprised you remember so many as you do. Well, they were from Troy. They were all from Troy. Yeah, and then through Heidahl. I drove Heidahl's teeth. I hauled bundles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how much of the year, let's say around 1910, how much of the year could you work out in the woods or in a harvest? How much work was there?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, about 30 days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For harvest… yes… what about woods? Woods work? How long could you work?

speaker: William Burkland
words: That's the year around, you know, woods work. That was the year around. If you wanted to be there all the time. The lumberjacks, that was the year around for them sometimes. In some camps. Well, put near all the camps. At that time when they used horses, that was the year. They didn't lay up for the winter. They wanted that snow. Snow and cold weather, that's what the lovers wanted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that means that in those days you could work for near as much as you wanted to. Yes, you could work as much as you wanted to. Put near head to, to me. Were you working the year round then yourself around 1910?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, I was there in the summertime in 1910. I was there one year or so, one summer. We worked at home. That's where we worked in the wintertime. We stayed home and worked in the log down here at the Mill, Dahlberg Mill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was that mostly, what were you doing on the place then? You were logging mainly or was that just winter? No, I was in the wintertime. We didn't log in the summertime. What else did you do on the place?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, we had, we raised hay and grain and cleared land and made wood. I hauled wood to Detroit along when I was about 14 years old. I started hauling wood to Detroit. We hauled about ten cords of wood to Detroit every winter for so long. I don't know how long. We hauled, I hauled wood every winter to Detroit, besides logging.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that trip like? What was that trip like, hauling the wood from Bear Creek, Detroit? What was it like?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah. Well, it was just like any other horse logging or horse work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean hauling it, hauling it in, what was the road like?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, the road was good in the wintertime. Oh, yeah. The road was real good in the wintertime. You know, they didn't do the roads then like they do now. They just, the horses were the ones that made the road. They tramped up the trail and they didn't, it wasn't icy like it is now. Now they scrape it, you know, scrape the snow off and it freezes ice. They go down to where there's moisture and probably below that. And then it freezes at night and you get a just a glare of black ice in some places. It wasn't that way then. It was just the road was tramped down by horses. And it was a nice, usually a real nice road. So your sled would run just as light as you please. And sleds would click, click, click all the time on the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much could you haul in one run?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, that all depended on how big a team you had. We had small horses, so I hauled about three quarters of a cord. Up to a cord, around a cord, if it was a nice dry wood where a cord is a load. That's what I figured.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much were you getting in Troy?

speaker: William Burkland
words: I kind of, I suppose it was around three dollars, I think so. Three dollars a cord.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a day's work to get down there and back? Yes, that was the day's work. It was long days too.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Not too long, it was about a ten hour day. Nine hours, anyway. It all depended on how good a team you had to walk. They walked. They walked what the team could walk. I never run my horses. I walked them. And that way they stood it every day. I could go every day. If you'd run your horse, you'd play them out. But I never did that. I walked my horses. But I didn't have, I had small horses and they didn't make as good a time as bigger horses did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say smaller, about what would they weigh?

speaker: William Burkland
words: One hundred and twelve, not over twelve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many did you have on the team?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Two. I had just two horses. Two horse teams in the wintertime. The person didn't like to drive four horses in the wintertime. Because it was up and downhill too much. Some places you had to roughlock, you know, to go down with a load.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you roughlock them?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, you hit a roughlock, maybe. And you hit what they called a roughlock lock on your chain. You had a chain you took under your runner. And then you run it back here and you had a lock. You had a double piece of iron that you run your chain through, the link. Then you had a ring up here that you dropped out on. When you got to the bottom, you went over there and kicked that ring off and went on. Your roughlock, it was on either the rack or the tray bar on your sledge. When you kicked that off, it was free. But then you take Troy Hill now. We had to roughlock that all the way down. We come in on the north side of Troy and come up on top of that road. And that was at one time, it was steeper than it is now by long shot. They've been grading that for over 80 years. They've got it pretty good now. Them days, you had ditches there right down. And I had a runaway team caught up with me down there. It hit my rig. I hauled lumber that time. And the one horse started up my tie board. When you hauled lumber, you hit a tie board. You put a twist on about the middle of your bumps. And then you let that tie board hang back. And it was getting slack by the time I got down to Troy. And it was laying right down on the load. And it bent down and put it near the ground. This runaway team caught up with me and one of them horses ran up that board and came up on the load and jumped off.

speaker: William Burkland
words: I had to run my team off the road and out in the snow. And they went on. But one of them horses got killed, it was just the same. He was the one that ran up the board. But the other horse pushed him into my load and he hit his shoulder against the load lumber before he ever jumped upon the board. And he skinned his whole shoulder so they had to shoot him. But they didn't stop there, they went on. They kept running. They went by. I got out of the way. So they went by and went down, clear down at the bottom of the hill they stopped down there. One of them crowded the other feller off the road and he fell down. So he was, they were laying there. One of them was laying down and the other was standing up. And the other was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These runaways were not too unusual in those days?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, they'd have quite a few runaways, but there wasn't too many of them. Not bad ones anyway, they'd probably get away a little ways. And some horses, some teams wouldn't run. They'd probably go back and then they'd tank left and stop. They wouldn't run. Some got awfully bad, you know. There's runaway horses. Now this one here that I'm telling about, that one of them horses was a runaway horse. And a runaway horse never quits. He's a runaway the rest of his life. He gets away a few times, but he never quits. Your horse never forget what he learned. He'll never, whether it's good or bad, it don't make any difference. He'll never forget what he learned. It's with him all the time. And he was a runaway horse, one of them, that's the one that pushed the other one in on the load. And he skinned his shoulder. The other horse wasn't hurt. The runaway horse never get hurt. But he'll hurt the other one every time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the reason why horses would run away? I mean how would they get started doing bad handling?

speaker: William Burkland
words: He might get scared the first time. He might get scared. And he might just get, you know, the horse, when he starts running, he scares himself. So then he scares himself. The harder he runs, the harder he will run before he gets used to it. And after that, why he don't get scared. Then he just runs away just because it's a habit. He never forgets it. It becomes a habit, you know, for a runaway horse to be a runaway. But in the first place he gets, probably gets scared and gets started. And after he gets started to run, anything he's got back up in, you know, makes an awful lot of noise and racket. Because they go pretty fast. They go about 50, 60 miles an hour. And the runaway horse, he runs pretty hard, you know, when he first starts out. And he scares himself up. And when you catch up with him, he might be standing there shaking. And he never forgets that. He just starts out any time he has a chance. After that he'll start out. He'll let a horse run away from you and he never forgets it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that there was a lot of difference in the way different people handled their horses… oh, sure.

speaker: William Burkland
words: It's a lot of difference. I let one team get away from me. And they run for about, oh, they run for about a big long mile anyway. And then one of them fell down, just couldn't make the turn. And he fell down and there was a, there was a fella that caught him and tied him up onto a tree there. And I found him. After that I walked up the road and I come to him. And he said, well, I think I met him too. And he asked me if that was my team. He says, it was my team and it was my fault. I said, did they do any damage to me? He said, it has plenty of damage. Your wood rack is laying all along the road. And your wagon is upside down. Well, I said they didn't kill anybody. No, they didn't kill him. I tied him to a tree down there. You'll find him there. I did too. They were tied to a tree. I took them and went home with them. And the next day we went, my dad and I went back and we picked up everything that we could that was any good. And I just made up my mind that's going to be the last time that I ever, because it was my fault to get away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they get away? How did it happen?

speaker: William Burkland
words: It just foolishly. I was going to water them. And in place of knowing where I had the lions, they were high life horses too. They were young horses. In place of knowing where I had the lions, I just dropped the lions and went over and unchecked them. See, we had checks on them. Oh, you strapped from the bit over there, came. I lied on and checked them, otherwise they couldn't drop their head down. And after I checked them, I went around and they drank and then they started up. Well, I howled, whoa, but they didn't stop. And I didn't know where the lions were. And God, they went right down. And they wished they took right off. Yeah, I couldn't. There was no use for me to run after them because they went to beat everything. And I just made up my mind that's the last time that they ever came to get away from me and I never had a runaway after that. And I was just a kid then, about 14, 15 years old. And I drove off horses for 40 years and after that never had a runaway. I had good, young, lively horses too all the time, well fed and everything. So they instead of got away, they had to run to beat everything. No, I tell you, a lot of that is all in the horsemanship. A lot of runaway. Sometimes you have an accident and probably fall off or you can't help it or anything like that. That's different. Anything as foolish as that runaway, that is all foolishness.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you, when you got to Troy, then in the middle of the day with your horses and that lumber, the cordwood, would you rest them there and spend some time in town?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah. Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do?

speaker: William Burkland
words: An hour, an hour all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do? How would you pass the time in Troy?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, I fed the horses. We fed them every noon in Troy. We had grain right with us and hay right with us on the load.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you?

speaker: William Burkland
words: And then I'd eat my dinner, whether I'd eat it at the restaurant or if I had lunch with me. Sometimes I'd have lunch and sometimes I'd eat the restaurant. And that's what we'd spend our time with. And we fed the horses. That was an hour every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you have time to visit friends in Troy?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, sure. Oh yes. We'd visit every day. Have a half hour runaway or more. Sometimes we stayed over an hour. If we had plenty of visiting, we stayed longer than an hour. Before we'd turn back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you know the people that were your friends in Troy? Were they people that had used to lived out here or people you'd met in town or where'd you know from? Oh, the people we met in town. We knew all the businessmen and practically all the Nora kids.

speaker: William Burkland
words: So there were plenty of visiting there to do. Oh yes. We knew Troy. It was just like Deary, you know. That's where we'd done our business. We was here for 20 or 25 years before there was any Deary or anything. It was all in Troy and Nora was all down there. All them young people at Nora was down at Troy all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Troy a very lively town in those days? Oh yes. That's better than it is now.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh yes. Troy was a lively town. Yes. It was really better than the Deary. Deary was more lumberjacks and toughies. Troy people. They were all farm kids and farm people and newcomers. So Deary, that was a hard town to begin with. All them old lumberjacks. They had a drink and a fight to eat everything. It was a rough town. I'll tell you that was roughy for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you see the fights in town and stuff when you were in Deary? Yeah. I seen fights all over.

speaker: William Burkland
words: They had some of them in town and they had some of them out the dances too. They were fighting. If they had some whiskey to drink for it, it usually turned out to a fight. That was nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I heard that lumberjacks were kind of tough fighters when they fought. They didn't like big boogers too.

speaker: William Burkland
words: They were hard ones. There was a knuckle mark on a door gazing up here for long, long, several years. They hit the wall. They said there was knuckle marks in there. They showed me some marks. I guess that was knuckle marks. The buildings them days were built out of green lumber. They couldn't knock a mark in some of these hard boards. Them days that lumber was green. It was possible to knock a mark in the board if they had a good big hard knuckle. Some of them were tough. There were big, long fellers who took a dig into the blow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard that if one lumberjack knocked another one down, that he might stomp with his cork boots.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah. I bet you life they went down flat on the ground too. Knocked out. Well that happened. It wasn't much of a fight if one of them didn't knock the other one down. Probably kicked the dick inside of them too. Oh yeah, they fought rough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know about the Marshall Hays getting shot in Troy when that happened?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think I was there the day he was shot.

speaker: William Burkland
words: I wasn't there just at that time, but I believe I was there that day. Yes, I knew Marshall Hays. He couldn't put me getting me one time. He was after me. I was just a kid of course and didn't know anything. We fed bundle hay. I don't know. I fed my horses down below that day. I fed them down below where I unloaded the wood. I unloaded the wood and I took part of the hay and went down there and fed them while I was unloading. Then I had another bundle of hay that I was going to give them after that. I fed them part of one bundle. Then I went back to uptown. Oh, I went up the... That's right. I left the sled or the wagon, whichever it was I had wood on. Then I went up the liver barn and bought a bundle of hay because they had already eaten up that first month. I went up there and bought another bundle of hay and I put that on the arm and started right down the sidewalk. Of course, hay is here. I was watching that and he was going to get me. I seen him. The liver barn was up there at Erickson's store. I seen him down there at the middle of town. I seen him coming. He was walking to beat everything and then he stopped and he looked. I seen him and then I thought about it. Maybe, by gosh, I can't... He was going to get after me because I'm on the sidewalk with a bundle of hay. So I crossed over and went over to the other side across there at the bank.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Then I went right down the street and he stood there and looked. He stood there and looked and he stopped and he stood there and looked. I was walking down the street with a bundle and he didn't come. But he was after me that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was against the law to have a bundle of hay on the sidewalk?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, you ain't supposed to walk on the sidewalk. It wasn't against the law. It was against their ordinance in order to walk on the sidewalk with anything that will put stuff on to the other pillar. If he gets too close to you and scatter here on the sidewalk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From what I've heard, Hayes was a pretty mean fellow. Oh, he was mean.

speaker: William Burkland
words: He was trying to make his wages on Fort Roy. He was working for Troy. He was trying to make his wages by prosecuting pillars that done anything wrong. Yeah, he was mean. Watching every chance he had to get money for Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I had the idea that people didn't like him. That people didn't like him because of arresting people all the time.

speaker: William Burkland
words: They didn't like him a bit. That's why he was shot. He was always after people more than he should have been. And then he got this after... What was it? Sly? Jigsaw. I think it was pain. Pain Sly. Yeah, I'm... Hayes. He caught my mother's cousin one time. And he heard nothing wrong with him. He had a drink. He was kind of lively. He felt a little lively. And probably wobbled a little. I don't know. He didn't. He was just as harmless as I told him. But he caught him and put him in jail too. It cost him five dollars to get out. That was five dollars for the town of Troy. Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, in Troy, they tell about how Joe Wells used to go drinking in Troy. And then Hayes would chase him and he'd get away.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah. Oh yeah, he was after. He was after everybody that drank. He drank a little bit while he was after. He had put me to everybody in jail down there. And find him. But then that was... I figured that was some of the judges too. The actual Olsen. He wanted to help Troy around with a long paper, the policeman. The actual Olsen was the judge then. That wasn't these actual Olsen. It's living now. That's the older set.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I heard that he got shot by Sly because he went up the hill. Sly was having a quarrel with his wife and told him not to come in. Yeah, well, I think he was called up there though.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Or took it upon himself to go up there. I don't know which. But then I think Sly was drunk too, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they were mad at Sly after that. I heard they had to get Sly out of town into Moscow pretty quick. Because some people were really... Some of Hayes's friends who were in the Oddfellows were...

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, I don't know. I don't know. I didn't know Payne Sly. I knew Jake Sly. He was a nice fella. I used to deal with Jake Sly. We used to trade horses. And I kind of liked him. I didn't know Payne Sly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, we were talking about Deary before. You mentioned to me, was it Roundtree who sold out at Deary before the town started? What was the story?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, he sold his place. He homesteaded. He homesteaded right in the middle of the town up here. But then he wasn't the only one. There were lots of them. He traded his place for a team to get out of here… that was Roundtree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just to have a team, he traded 160… yeah.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, that 160 wasn't worth more than about $100. You would buy mostly... If you'd have had the money in the early days, you'd buy $160 for $100. Some of them, you know, they got anxious to go. They'd been here and couldn't see no future here. You know, you had to be a working man. You had to be a foreigner. If you see any future in this country, them days. It's just like getting down here in the woods and sit there and no markets. This is nothing. You had that 160-acre and nothing done on it. If you cut down the trees, well, you had all stumps to get rid of. I'll tell you, it was a hard thing to buck. That took a bunch of Swedes and Germans and foreigners to see through that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why foreigners and not Americans? Why would foreigners be more willing to do it? Why would foreigners be more willing to do it than the Americans? They were used to it.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Now my folks and all their other Swedes, they came from Sweden. Sweden was really nothing but rock and timber. That's all it was, especially where my dad came from. It was nothing but rock and timber. And all they lived off of that. They didn't even use the song that they chopped them off. Chopped one end square and the other one back. They usually stood on the log. Now he could hit. My dad could hit. He let us straight as a board almost. They say they can't hit the same mark twice, but he could do that. And he chopped one end with square cut and the other one was beveled on both sides. That's the way he chopped logs. Sometimes they stood on the, if they had a smaller timber, they'd stand up on the log on the water. Because everything was drove there, you see, drove by water. That's the way they moved their timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I was thinking when Potlatch came into the country, didn't that open up a good market for people to make money with their timber?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yes, but then, we've been here for 25 years before they came. We came here and, like, then fellers up here, they homesteaded here in the early 80s, or middle 80s anyway. And Potlatch didn't get here until 1905, and then it was nothing new. And then for two or three years, three or four years. So you can figure that. 80s, 1985, 1905 is when they moved. And when the railroad come, and then it was about five years after that, the market was out, it was 25 years. The people were here and there was absolutely nothing. It was just, they had to make their way, and it was a trading chance, and those that wasn't close to anywhere, what made it better down there where we live was Pete Delbert. You were telling me about him. Yeah, he was really the making of the country down there. He would trade for anything, anything. He didn't make any difference, and you could trade him anything you wanted for anything he had. So they could trade with him. And those that could get in touch with him, they had some chance to trade stuff and also get stuff. He had that sawmill and he had lumber, and that was needed badly because they didn't have any lumber before. So he traded lumber for anything they had. They didn't make any difference. If you wanted to trade, you would trade with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When Potlatch came in, how good was the money that they gave for the timber? Did they get it cheap or did they get it dear?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh yeah, they get it cheap. There were two dollars a thousand. Two dollars a thousand was all they paid. So people didn't get rich selling their timber to Potlatch either. They didn't get rich, but they got something to work with. Just the same. You know you could buy a lot for a dollar or two. Fifty cents for a pair of overalls, now they're eight dollars a pair. Well, you bought them all you wanted for fifty cents a pair. Three dollars for a pair of shoes, now they're twenty-five. That was a big difference too. It didn't make any difference how little money you had. If you had any money, you could buy anything. That's the reason that they could trade a team for 160 acres. That 160 acres wasn't worth it, wasn't about a hundred dollars. They got all that was on it usually, was a log cabin. They didn't figure the time to put that up. Some had doors and windows in it and some didn't. So there you are, but then I think Roundtree had a door and window in his. It was that way for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like nobody could really get ahead.

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, they didn't get ahead and it took time to get ahead. They could get ahead, it wasn't that. But they had to get rid of that timber to get any ground. Then after they got rid of the timber, they had to get away of the stump that came from that timber. Then after that, about ten years or so, probably they could get rid of a stump. Unless they were used to dynamiting, then they could blow out a stump. But they didn't do that. These fellers that came here, they saw down the timber and burnt that up. They felled trees like them there. They bored in through the heart. Then they bored down through the heart and they met. Then they dropped fire down in there and them trees were pitchy. They started burning and they'd lay there all winter and burn. Well, they'd burn off until there was nothing but a shell. Then they'd saw or chop that shell off and get rid of it. Now that was a slow progress. And hard work too. But they didn't care for that, they were used to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many acres could you clear in a year? In the amount of land you would clear up in a year of work on a place? That's a hard question. It's hard to say.

speaker: William Burkland
words: That all depends on how good a worker you was and how much time you put in at it. So that was hardly hard. I tell you, it took about 50 years before they had about 40 or 50 acres. It averaged about an acre a year. In about that time they had about most of them unless they went at it. They devoted most of their time at doing that. They'd have about 40 or 50 acres cleared in about 40 or 50 years. That's about the way that worked out. But that wasn't the hardest part of it. They had to work down in the first 20 years. They had to sit here in a homestead. They couldn't leave the place either because then they'd jump them. They couldn't leave the place. Then they'd have to walk to Genesee on a Sunday evening and walk back. You walk on on Monday or Sunday evening and walk back on Saturday night. They had to come home too and change clothes and be with their family. That's the way they then worked for a dollar a day, maybe a dollar and a half. Well, a dollar a day. They worked for less than that to begin with, the first years.

speaker: William Burkland
words: But then that dollar would buy more than $10 for buying out. So it brought us along. But they tried to hold them up there on the town side. They tried to buy there, but them fellas, they tried to hold them up a little too high. Then they came up here and started the town here. They had already bought this. That's the reason the town is in Deary, otherwise it would have been down here, Dave on. That's where they figured on building it too. But them fellas there, they had the medals and they figured they had cultivated ground. Everything was ready there and they tried to hold them up on the price a little higher. That didn't work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Deary, the site of Deary, timbered before the Potlatch came in? Before the town started, was it timber on the site? It hadn't been developed or farmed or anything. No, no, no, it was all timber. That was logged.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Snyder, a fellow by the name of Snyder logged Deary. He had a logging. He had a contract to log. He logged a whole thing around here. So that was done after Deary started. There was a little clearing there. There was a fellow by the name of Blalock that had a homestead there. He had about five or six acres cleared around here. So that's really where they started, was on that clearing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's all that was cleared, about five, six acres. Well, that's all that was cleared.

speaker: William Burkland
words: That's all that was cleared. It's just enough to prove up on. You had to have so much kind of cleared. It didn't have to be that, to prove up the place. I think it was five acres. I think that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many springs right there?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah. There was water coming up there. It was nothing. It wasn't springs. It was just seepage. But it was water in the ground, because that was something that flowed from the hill on down. You know, if you had a slope where there was timber back of you, where there was always water draining down there, they didn't find any water in Deary, in less than 150 feet. And there wasn't much. They never found more than so much water in Deary. That's the reason they couldn't get water in Deary. So much, and that was 150 feet. They had to go down to that, and it wasn't enough for a town. So they had to go back on east of town, the next draw, Brush Creek, they call it. Arthur Burke lives on Brush Creek. And that's where they found the water. That's where they got the water. There was a geologist here who found that part. But Deary had so much water, and they went down, they thought they'd go down deeper and find more, but they had to build 1 ,000 feet, I think. Five, six hundred and 1 ,000 feet. They didn't find any more. There wasn't any more to be had. Then the geologists told them, there's no use to it. You won't find any more. They found so much. They found that in any place where they dug, they found it 150 feet. So much water, but it wasn't enough. It was enough for a family or something like that, but not for a town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the town go up there fast? When it started building the town, did it kind of spring up overnight?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, but it went up pretty fast for the first part of the town. It went up pretty fast. I don't know. In about five years, it was bigger than it is now. There was more building. Of course, it wasn't very substantial, anything. Just a number of buildings. But it went up that fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd heard Jergensen was the first one to start a business in town there.

speaker: William Burkland
words: He was the first one. He was one of the first ones. Jergensen was the first one to have a store in there. But then there was a Peterson, Ben Peterson, he had the hardware store in there from Troy. No, he lived in Troy. He started the hardware store. He moved up here and started the hardware store. I don't know whether that was the same year. It was near all of them started the same year. The following year, it was in the fall when the railroad came in. When in the world did they sell them lots? With Jergensen. No wonder that... They were selling lots in 1908. I know that. There were selling lots in 1907. They had a lot. Of course, they sold before that. But they had an auction sale on lots in the fall of 1907. I don't know. I really don't. Yeah. But Jergensen started before that. I think he started a building in 1970. But then you see he bought from the townside company. He picked his lot up before they ever started selling. Then after they got kind of squared out and got all the surveying done, then they had an auction sale on lots. That's when Dad bought 40 acres there. That was in the home and orchard tracks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of these store people come from Troy and Nora?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, they did. Jergensen was here. He didn't come from Troy. He came from Nora. He had a store in Nora. He moved that up there. That's halfway between here and Troy. He came from there. Ben Peterson came from Troy. He started a hardware store. Eric Anderson started a jewelry store. He also came from Troy. John Celine started a saloon. He also came from Troy. Then Melacol, he got the post office. I think he started a confectionary before he got there. He was a farmer down here, Dave Hahn. And Swann Erickson was from Troy, too. Swann Erickson was from Troy. He came from Troy. Swann Erickson had blacksmiths all around here before that. He was here with the early settlers. He started blacksmithing. He first started blacksmithing down here at the Anderson post office. That's where Ted Klett lived now. He was blacksmith there.

speaker: William Burkland
words: He and Schroberg, they moved up this way about half a mile. That's right south of Reuben Johnson on the hill there. You know where Reuben Johnson lived? On the hill there, there was a fellow named Schroberg. He had 40 acres there. They had moved that blacksmith shop up there and headed there a while. Then Swann Erickson moved back to Boo down to Norah. He had put up a blacksmith shop there. After that, he moved to Troy. Then after the dearest got to going, he moved up here and built a good blacksmith shop there. He had quite a good one in Troy, too. Troy Lumber Company wanted to buy him out there. Then he sold to them. He had his blacksmith shop right there at the Troy Lumber Company's office. That's where Swann Erickson had his blacksmith shop in Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a guy was Swann Erickson?

speaker: William Burkland
words: He was a good guy, very good guy. Really too good for his own use. He wasn't easy, he wasn't too awfully easy, but then he was an awful good guy. Very nice stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say too good for his own use, you thinking of being maybe too kind to people?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, he'd give up and let him give him a lot of things that he probably wasn't entitled to. He was very good. He'd argue too. He'd give in and he'd help him along. If that is, he didn't insist on him that he'd help him or he'd do that. He was an awful good guy, Swann Erickson. I've heard he really liked the kids. Oh yeah, he liked kids. He'd give them anything that they wanted. Anything they wanted, he'd give it to them. Yeah, he said if they wanted something, they'd come and ask him for it. Oh yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. You go get it out of paperwork. He was just a good brother, that's all. Then he had a horse. He bought a horse. It was about half broke when he got it. He was no horseman at all. The kids broke that horse for him, I think. They wrote him. At first they couldn't write him. He'd buck him up. Then there was a circuit writer who came up here, a preacher. He used to be a circuit writer. I don't know why that horse bucked. It might have been that she was kind of ticklish on the cinch, you know, cinch-bound, they called it. Because when they strapped the saddle on her, she'd start bucking. This preacher, he was a circuit writer. He wasn't a prairie. He wanted to borrow that horse from Spondin' and ride out someplace out here in the country. He could have the horse, but he says, I don't know, you can ride him. He says, I'll try. He says, I'll buck you up. The preacher said, that's all right. He was a pretty young man then.

speaker: William Burkland
words: He put the saddle on him and got in the saddle and started him out. The horse went right up in the air and bucked. He bucked himself down. He bucked until he fell down. The preacher just jumped out of the saddle when he started falling. He just jumped out of the saddle and stood by the horse. Then the horse got down and pretty soon he laid there and wanted to get up. He got up and the preacher, he was riding the saddle again. He was in the saddle before the horse got up. He said, you think you're heading up? He said, let's go for a ride. He started kicking along and started him out. The horse never bucked after that. He never bucked after that. He bucked so hard, he went down and jumped. His hind legs doubled up and he went down. The preacher, he jumped out of the saddle. When the horse started going up, he was in the saddle again. He was a pretty clever man, that's for the reason. He rode all his life.

speaker: William Burkland
words: He knew what he was doing. He wasn't afraid of the horse. Then after that, everybody rode him. The Olsen girls, Emmy Olsen had four or five girls. They'd all ride him at the same time. He rode him from ear to tail. They were all riding that thing. Two or four of them girls were on the horse and riding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Joe Wells? Did you know Joe? Joe Wells?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh yes, I knew Joe Wells. I knew Joe Wells all the time. I think he was here probably before we was. Not very long, but probably a year or so before we was. I knew him all the time. I stayed there. I used his barn one winter. He was around there all the time. He was an ice fella. I liked Joe Wells. We logged up there on the Anderson Place and we used his barn. He was around there too. I think he was logging someplace that winter too. I knew him and his wife too. Roy was a real nice hero. Chuck was all right too, but Chuck would drink quite a little. He would get kind of mean sometimes. He got in trouble every once in a while. A little bit. Not much. Joe Wells would drink too. But he wouldn't get in trouble so much. He'd get a little loud and he'd tell him to shut up. Well, I know my room was little.

speaker: William Burkland
words: He'd shut up… the whisker would work on him. Chuck, he was worse. He'd get mad and want to fight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did a lot of people visit over at their place? Would a lot of people visit over at their place? The Wellses… yeah.

speaker: William Burkland
words: The Potlatch people, that is those that came, you know, the first ones. That was really the head crew, engineers and the surveyors. They'd eat there. They'd always stop for dinner with Joe Wells. Especially if they got orders in the head so they knew they were coming. They'd eat there at Chuck Cross or Joe Wells' place. Yeah, they run quite a little lodging there when they really built the railroad. They made a little money there then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they, would you say that their family was just as accepted as any other family in the early days here?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yes, yes, absolutely. They were with them on everything around here when they were younger. Both the boys and Mary, the girls, they were on every dance and everything. Even the old people, they were just as welcome as anybody else. I don't know of any place that they wasn't welcome.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that changed a little as time went on and the country changed and the town got in here? Do you think that they were more together with the people in their early days? Oh, yeah, they were always together. But what I'm asking is, do you think that changed as time went on?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, no, I don't think it changed much. But the only change is, it did differently from what they did when they were in the early days. It changed in that way that they don't group together like they did in the early days. They grouped together, you know, they'd always go into a place, never have the fun at the neighbor and visit at the neighbor. Now you go to town, you visit in town and place, out in the country. We didn't do that, but we were right out in the country. Neighborhood. So it would change that way. But I don't think the people changed much. They were about the same as the others was. But the visiting part of it is a little different than it was. You know, we'd go to a neighbor on Saturday evenings. Some places we'd dance and some places we'd play cards and some places we'd do this and that. That all depended on where you went and how they wanted it.

speaker: William Burkland
words: But now if you want to do anything like that, you've got to go to town. So it differs that way, but otherwise I don't understand that you can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you visited the neighbors, would it just be the two families?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh no. There'd be several of them, three or four families together, usually. And maybe more. Some places, you know, they'd have a dance. And there'd be a whole lot of, both young and old people. If that is, if they had room, some of these people put up a big building, you know, and they had got a little room. And there'd be three, four, five, six families together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And dinner? Food? Eats?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, they'd always have a, they'd always bring me and have a lunch together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you, did you know the Hanson brothers that made the coffins?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yes, yes, I knew them. Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard they were really good at work with wood. They were very inventive people. Yes, they were.

speaker: William Burkland
words: They had a sawmill up here too. Up there on the east of Helmer. They had a sawmill there. They were pretty handy with wood, woodwork. Yes, I knew them. I forget the name. It was Carl and Herman. I've just heard them called the Hanson brothers. That's what I've heard. Yeah, well, they called them the Hanson brothers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, did you know, did you know Malcher Anderson? Malcher?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yes. There was one Malcher and one Malkus. But they, they wasn't brothers, of course. Axel's brother. I'm thinking of Axel, Axel Anderson's brother, Malcher. I didn't know him very well. I knew Ebbings. I didn't know him very well. I knew Axel Anderson better than I did his brother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work for him?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No. I didn't work for Axel Anderson. No, I didn't. I didn't work for him. They backfired from the houses in the homestead days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They used to set fires.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, they backfired. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the idea there?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Fire, fire breaks. So that the fire won't, if you backfire from your house, the fire that came in won't come any further than up to that burn. That stopped them. They backfired. They said they started the fire around their own place where they could hold it, you know, and backfired against the other fire. And when it came up to where the other one had burnt, there was nothing to burn. So it stopped there. That was what they called backfiring.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did homesteaders do that every year?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, if the fire had come, otherwise they wouldn't. If there was a fire coming, there was nobody put out a fire. They had to start a fire way back maybe, and, well, everything started a fire. That was started by prospectors, miners, campers, Indians, and homesteaders. Anybody started the fire, and nobody put it out. It burnt that little went out. And if it came in on the other feller, he'd backfire from his own place and let it burn. It was nothing but the oil grass, if there was any left. And in the fall of the year, of course, after the grass got matured, it burned, too. But the green grass don't burn. And after it burnt, it wasn't over, oh, say, a week, the green grass started to come up again. That burning don't hurt grass any, where it burns all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you're saying that the homesteaders didn't care very much if there was a fire? That didn't make no difference.

speaker: William Burkland
words: It didn't bother them any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It didn't burn a timber?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, no, no, no, no, there's no timber to burn. It's just big trees. What burns the timber is, your big trees, they shed bark. And you leave that bark for about, you leave a tree there without a fire around it for ten years, and you've got that much dry bark. That's what kills your tree, because that gets awfully hot. That's almost like coal. But where fire goes every year, they wanted the fire to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean there used to be fires that often? Maybe every year there'd be a fire?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, every year, every year. In the early days, that is before we came, when the country was wild, lightning would set fire. And there'd be something that set fires, and prospectors, you know, there'd been prospectors here all the time. And campers, and everything, Indians, Indians come here and went through here and went back in the mountains, went back here on white rock and picked huckleberries. Well, they'd all start a fire, and nobody ever put a fire out. They just left it, let it burn. It burned out. It'd have burned out by itself, or otherwise, well, it'd have to burn out by itself, because they'd never put it out. Or maybe rain would come. Yeah, rain, yes, hadn't put it out. Yeah. Because otherwise it didn't burn when it came to where it'd been burnt before, there's nothing to burn, it went out. And it wasn't, green grass didn't burn, it's nothing but old grass that burnt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'll leave when you got...

speaker: William Burkland
words: You were young, doing exchange work from one place to the other. Sure, exchange work. Everybody did that. You got that in with the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ed Swenson?

speaker: William Burkland
words: He talked about that, yeah. Yeah, that's all true. Everything he... I've read that book, it's all true, everything he says. That's the way we get along. There was just exchange work, there was nobody hiring, nobody had any money, it was just... There was nothing to get money for. It was all exchange, that's why... That's why Dahlberg was such a help for this country, because he'd exchange for anything. He was really in the business, he was in the business, that business before he ever come to this country. He was a trader, a peddler. He used to... He was one of them, they called him... And he was a peddler, you know, that went around the country with a peddling bag on the shoulder of him. Before he...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back where? Minnesota?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, back in Sweden. His father was before him. He was a peddler. He'd carry the back sack and went around the country and peddled. That's where Dahlberg got his... That's where that come from, that he was such a trader. Now I'll tell you, Dahlberg was the biggest help for this country that ever could have got here. Because anything that he had, he didn't have groceries or anything like that, or food stuff. Well, he had food stuff in the wild, or tame animals. Yeah, trade it for anything. Pigs, chickens, horses, cows. He'd trade lumber for it, and they all had lumber after he started the sawmill away. That was the only place they could get lumber. They'd come all the way from Uniontown up here and get lumber. Trade them colts, horses for it. There was one feller who came with four colts. He'd got three of them. He'd come up here with six horses and four colts. Before he left, I think he was supposed to stay overnight. When he left, I... He'd already had three of them colts. Sold them to their neighbors around here. Trade them to them, maybe. I don't know how he sold them. If there was any money, he'd get it, of course. He'd sell it cheap enough so they could buy it. The other few dollars, he'd sell it cheap enough so they could buy it. He'd drink up the money, too. Good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When after Deary started, after the town came in here, how often would you go to town from Bear Creek there?

speaker: William Burkland
words: We'd get up there every day after Deary. Yeah, we'd go anytime we wanted. That's a little way of standing. We'd drive the team and hack for four or five. We drove the team and hack for four or five. Well, from 1905, when Deary started, to 1916. We got the first car in 1916. Before that, I had a one-horse buggy and drove all over the country here with that. We could drive as far as... We couldn't get around like we can now. We couldn't take off and go a long way. We'd limit ourselves to what a horse could do. But we always had four or five horses. I had a one or two very good single horses. See, our horses wasn't very big. Them days, they were around the 1100s, up to 12. I don't know whether we ever had a 1200-pound horse until I got them coats that I broke. Little horses didn't hurt them to travel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, once the town started, you were saying that it kind of changed and people did more in town and less in the neighborhood. Did that take a long time to change like that, or did it change right away?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, that didn't change. It didn't take very long. Until they got their buildings... Oh,… i don't know. Maybe two or three years. See, when Ben Peterson came up here, he built the hardware store. Then he put in upstairs there, he put in a skating rink. Well, then of course the people went up there. The young people went up there, the skating rink. Then they started baseball games. Well, that took the people up here to town. That started as soon as... putting here as soon as the dairy, a year or two after dairy started. Then the whole brooks came in here and started confectionaries. Then they wanted the people to come up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about dance? Were there dances in town too then… oh, yeah… where did they have the dances?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, they had it in the skating rink. They had the skating rink in Danzwel. That was in the Ben Peterson hardware store, the Ben Peterson building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then there was less get-togethers out in the country here. There was less neighborhood get-togethers.

speaker: William Burkland
words: You said you used to dance in people's homes, and I guess then they stopped doing that. That kind of divided that up after the town started. So then if you had to dance out in the country, you wouldn't have probably more than just a few neighbors got together and had to dance. They didn't have any public dance. There was no public then. Otherwise, if they had a... The dances that they had in that mountain, in the wintertime, there'd be just a few neighbors put together probably and had a party of their own. But before that, for dances, they had pavilions. They called them pavilions. They built the pavilions outside. There were several of them. There was one down here on the Taylor Place. They had a pavilion. My brother was in all that. They built a pavilion. That was on the Potlatch Place. They had a baseball diamond. And they built a pavilion, dancing pavilion. Was that temporary just for the summer, or would it be for... Yeah, it was for the summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think I've heard that called the Bowery.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, there's some of them called the Bowery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it the same thing?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, it's the same thing. They called it the Dance Pavilion or the Bowery. So they'd build it...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would they build it out of?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, they'd build it out of lumber. That is after... Dahlberg started that mill in the early 90s. That's where they got the lumber. Yeah, he posted that here, not me. It was a simple little luncheon. He had a sawed lumber and he had a planer, too. He sized the lumber and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's what happened. So it was just temporary, these pavilions, just for the summer, and then they'd take them down or would they leave them? Yeah, they'd borrow the diamonds.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, I don't know. They'd leave them over with yours, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that you?

speaker: William Burkland
words: You.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About Deary and when it started to go downhill. Because you said it used to be a lot bigger and a better town than it is nowadays. No, I don't know.

speaker: William Burkland
words: I don't know. Well, you see, there used to be a harness shop and three... There was three taverns, but I don't know when it started to go down. And a hotel. Two hotels. A doctor and dentist. It started to go down when the logging was done around here. That's about what started the most of that go down. See, that was... Oh, I don't think that was... When did they log here last? Remember what year?

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: They volunteered in 1916 on this place.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, it's 1820. I think they were down on Bear Creek in the 20s. Oh, about 1930, I'd say. It started to slowly go down. We didn't go down to where it is now, but then it went down. I'll take the dishes. Two blacksmith shops.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: How would you care for...?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, no, that's fine. Thank you. Two blacksmith shops. There was at one time two blacksmith shops. And two or three garages. A repair shop, of course. And a hotel. A harness shop.

speaker: William Burkland
words: And a shoe shop. And I don't know, there was two or three or four, whichever it was, they called them near-beer joints. That was taverns, you know. And there was two pool halls. What was it, a motorized? It's quite a bit. And a telephone. What did you call them? What do you call them? What was it? A telephone?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Exchange.

speaker: William Burkland
words: What did you call them? That telephone they had here? Here?

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Just a telephone exchange. Just a telephone exchange. Yeah, but then they all had it.

speaker: William Burkland
words: What do we call them? Well, anyway, telephone office. We call them telephone office, that's the word. See, they had kind of a central here, telephone central. They'd call it Troy, and exchange, or so good and there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, let me ask you, Bill, how much do you think that Deary, the town, changed this country around? Do you think it had a huge effect, or some effect, or... Did it change the country around a great deal, the way people lived?

speaker: William Burkland
words: It changed it in one way. It brought in more money. Of course, that would have come anyway, I suppose. It brought in more money. See, there's more money. The Potlatch lumber company brought in more money here. They made more cash. That's where the change was, mostly. It brought in more cash. I don't know if it had been like it was when we first came here. It had been pretty slow. They had mills here, but they didn't make enough money. Potlatch come in here with money, and their mill, they started that Potlatch mill. That brought in a lot of money, too. We noticed that around here, too, the Potlatch business down there, that Potlatch mill. I have an idea that made the change.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I get the idea, though, that Potlatch got the timber here almost for nothing. They just walked off with it. I know, but that doesn't make any difference. There was a lot of timber here.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Boy, there was a lot of timber here. We got part of the bulk. We got a part of the bulk that's taken out of here. There wasn't much value in the timber. But, every little bit helped. They had timber claims, the best timber claims. But they sold for only about $1 ,200 and $1 ,500. That's what the good timber claims is. They got $1 ,500, and they didn't have $1 ,500 before. So there you are. It all goes together. Now, my mother had an awful good claim. That's all. Or she got a little more, but Dad got $1 ,500 for his. He had a claim.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were their claims located?

speaker: William Burkland
words: My mother's claim was located just east of Bovill, over on, let's see, between Potlatch and Elk River. That's where my mother's claim was located. My dad's, I don't know where in the world that was. That was further back. He didn't get that much money either. He got about, I think, around $1 ,000 for his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she go out there and live there on it?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, they didn't. They proved that up with money. $400. Yes, they didn't live on them. Not on the stone and timber claim. This is the homestead that they had to live on. That was supposed to be a home. But not on the stone and timber. They took up what they called the stone and timber. And they proved that up with $400.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was thinking, do you think that the fires in Deary had much to do with the town going down? Do you think the fires hurt the town very much?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, I don't think so. The buildings that they wanted to do went up quick. That they wanted to go up. And then they built another better building. You mean when the town burnt? I don't think that hurt neither the people nor the town very much. Them fires, you can't never tell about them. They start, and they say, oh, this is accidental and all that. Sometimes they want them to burn. So you can't.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: The fire was not in the 20s?

speaker: William Burkland
words: It was in the 20s, she says.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard of that in other towns for the insurance money.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Guys would burn down their buildings for the insurance money. Sure, they want them to burn some of them. If their business slacks out. And they have got a cheap building, what's the user holding back if they've got... I don't think that much they are. Well, if a person doesn't know that, that's something that held secret. And if a person never finds anything about that, what started the fire?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in Troy, they caught, there was a Knutzen-Hennings saloon burned down. And they caught them. They had a deal about burning the place down.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, well, they didn't catch these. Of course, I don't know whether they... The fire started during the night and what started it, that's another one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get out and fight on that fire?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, I was out there, sure. I was out there, but then it was going to beat everything. There was no fire department either. It just bucketed the chance. Two men at the pump, pumping, and we carried water in the bucket. There was no that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a line, a bucket brigade?

speaker: William Burkland
words: No, I don't know. There wasn't a line at all. There wasn't that much buckets around there by the time I got there, because I got there pretty early. I was living in Wrighty and Deary, in one fire. And the other fire, I was living down here on the farm, the first fire. I was living down here on the farm. That was three miles to come. That was burnt up before I ever got there. And on the tail end of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this fire when you were in Deary, how much burnt down that time? What happened?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Oh, I don't know. It is quite, I think Main Street. Practically everything on the west side, but the brick building burnt down. Because I know the harsh head of the bank, and he was out there. He was a peg-legged feller. He was out there and he says, we've got to save the bank. We've got to save the bank. Otherwise we'd be up against it. We've got to save that. Of course, they went in and saved that. The fire was coming to beat everything, but they carried water and held it back. So they saved the bank as a brick building. There was a fella there that was pretty heck. He was all full of the dickens, anyway. He said, harsh was out there. He said, we've got to save the bank. We've got to save the bank. When it was over with. And he said, I saved my bank, anyway. And really the others that saved the bank.

speaker: William Burkland
words: He always said, hen. He used that by word. He said, oh, hen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was Fred Heck? Fred Heck did that?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Hars did that. Hars, he'd always say, hen. There was anything that went wrong. He used that by word. Oh, hen. He says, I saved my bank, anyway. After he'd been begging everybody to help save the bank. But it wouldn't have burnt, anyway, because it was a brick building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Fred Heck was just making fun of him.

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah, he was making fun of him. Fred Heck, he'd always listen to things like that, you know, and hear them things. He didn't make fun of him. Fred Heck, he was always full of fun. He could have any fun while he was right there. No, that time the bank, or the town burnt all the way from Jurgis and Storff, clear up the bank. All the buildings. But that was the last fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to celebrations in Deary… yeah… like what did they celebrate there?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Fourth of July. They had celebrations every year. The first celebration was the fourth of July. Then they had a big celebration. And the second one was fair, I think. They had a fair. That's the second celebration.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A fair like a town fair, like a county fair would be?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Yeah. Yeah, because it didn't amount to that much, but then it was what it amounted to, anyway. They had a fair. They brought in stuff that they raised on the farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that strawberry festival? Did they ever have that in the earlier days? Oh, yeah. They had that for several years.

speaker: William Burkland
words: They had a lot of them strawberry festivals. But then, you know, that kind of petered out, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would that be, the strawberry festival?

speaker: William Burkland
words: Well, it's just a get together, something like a fair. And then they'd have strawberries to sell. And, yeah, that's about all it amounted to. And then they'd come to a celebration.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So when you were grown up, let's say in the teens, in the 20s, and even in the 30s, mostly what you did was work on the home place, on the farm. Yeah. And you farmed it?
